_generated: Auto-generated from GU_HOOKS.md by scripts/generate-hooks-json.ts. Do not edit by hand — edit the markdown source and re-run.

rumors.text: A "geometry-quiet" route has been profitable for two captains and fatal for the third.
A retired observerse mathematician is selling the only working copy of a calibration she ran in '87 — from a system since quietly removed from every chart.
A station nineteen months "gone strange" stopped answering last shift; the auditor sent to map the seep beneath has not checked in.
Three syndicates have already pre-sold the harvest from a bloom that hasn't opened yet — and the flare it depends on is already a day late.
An intensity band reads "quiet" on every public chart and "major" on a leaked corporate one.
An ore lot assays clean as ice until the chiral instrument lights up, and the manifest is now a felony in three systems and a windfall in two.
A weather-watcher on an aerosol-rich moon sells flare-history readings to anyone who'll port — and three of her customers shouldn't know each other.
A cult ritualizes the Blade as "honest weapon" — no shrapnel, only the cut intended; their last livestream is being passed hand-to-hand.
A navy lab posted a Blade-tech vacancy and quietly closed it again the next shift.
An anchor-mass freighter sale just hit the open market and three syndicates are quoting interception windows.
Doped ore is moving through customs on a manifest that lists it as "ballast"; the inspector who signs it retires next quarter.
Zen-garden lines are appearing in a station's lower decks that nobody raked.
A pirate ambush zone famous for sensor ghosts has gone quiet — pirates moved or hit something faster than themselves.
Calibration scars at a "clean" merchant lane match a wanted ship's drive signature.
A pilot has new math for pinching where his sister vanished, and he's hiring half-shares.
A "stable wake" used as a nav aid for thirty years is in fact a failed-gate scar; the late arrivals are getting older.
An anchor mass audit was forged; the consortium is racing to top up before the next heavy transit.
A coded warning beacon was finally decrypted by a freelance cryptographer — and her transcript is being faked four directions.
A surgical strike took a "fishing platform" that had no business being a target.
A merchant's count of Silence Days disagrees with the official one by four; he insists he heard a broadcast nobody else logged.
An order of monks count days since rain in days since broadcast; their abbey is on a moon the Gardener has never struck.
A rogue ASI fragment in an outer-belt habitat is offering navigation help to passing ships — three captains have taken it, two are still alive.
A stabilizer-substrate vein has been found on a moon with no chiral signature, which should be impossible.
A first-wave probe launched pre-Silence is due back this year on its original return arc.
A retired surveyor is selling the location of a ruin she found and never reported; the percentage to her grandchildren is high.

contracts.text: Triple rate to charter an "observerse-quiet" detour around a system flagged for active geometry research; the buyer is a university nobody's heard of.
Be there first when the forecast bloom opens; three syndicates have pre-sold the harvest, and the ship you're flying isn't on their books.
Sample a drifting river before it reaches a Gardener warning beacon; nine days to assay, and the broker won't be at the rendezvous if you're late.
Move calibrated medical stock to an exposure outbreak through a customs cutter that "needs to verify the assay"; every hour of verification is a coffin.
Verify a vein whose handedness flips on a 40-day cycle — first known periodic chiral source if she's right, and she's been right twice in a row.
Quietly remove a Shiab Blade emitter from a decommissioned hull before the auditors arrive; payment in clean registry transfers, emitter still warm.
Recover a Blade prototype from a corporate cutter wreck that hasn't drifted in nine years, sitting inside a quiet bleed node — which is itself a problem.
Disappear an anchor-mass lot between buyers; the lot's previous owner is the audit committee chair, and the next buyer doesn't know.
Survey a moon whose forty-day rotation makes doped ore weigh measurably more on the long nights — two crews already lost on the wrong side.
Clear runaway growth from an outpost six weeks late on its call-in; the contract didn't specify how to extract the inhabitants.
Deliver inhibitor grit before the next shift, and you become the new road authority — the freight monopoly will pay you to fail.
Escort a freighter through a known shear lane and bring back hull-slice photographs to match — or contradict — a competitor's denied claim.
Recover a corporate yacht ten light-seconds inside a navy interdiction; the navy can salvage in eighteen hours, you have nineteen, and a tug is already auctioning for the difference.
Tune a refinery to a route nobody legally flies; the only refinery that'll touch the job is on a Gardener-flagged station.
Ferry an heir to a port council meeting tomorrow that voids a freight monopoly; the monopoly needs the heir to arrive late, and the only inbound is yours.
Inspect a working gate's anchor mass and don't file a report; the consortium is paying very well, the heir is paying better.
Deliver an audit team to the wrong dock; a captain at the checkpoint has been skimming compliance line items for six months.
Drop a probe just past the exclusion zone; payment is in advance and the contract specifies no return clause.
Charter run toward Sol with paperwork older than the Silence; the heir is hiring crew one at a time so no one knows the manifest.
Crack a witness core that's been sealed since a salvage-court scandal; one side wants it cracked, the other wants the cracker dead, and you're being paid by neither.
Recover a stabilizer substrate convoy from a "salvage dispute" — whoever controls it controls eighteen months of every AI in the sector.
Retrieve a colony charter intact from inside an outpost being slowly rebuilt by self-ordering regolith; the contract is for the paperwork, not the people.
Recover a first-wave archive cached on a moon nobody charted; the cache key is in a song the heirs still teach their children.

encounters.text: Long-exposure cases from a black-market node are turning up in core-world clinics; somebody with a manifest gets charged with murder, and somebody with paperwork makes sure it's the wrong somebody.
A "window" port's safe-interval table is being subtly forged shift by shift; a competitor's freighters keep arriving early to empty berths.
A passenger with quiet contamination is bleeding wrong-handedness onto every surface in the galley.
Two refineries are running half capacity because somebody mis-painted a hatch and let mixed-handedness plating onto the same hull; the recall is quiet, the buyback is desperate.
A Shiab-math instability is closing a profitable lane; a navigator selling "stable shift" predictions is right often enough to be dangerous and wrong often enough to bury freighters.
A coastal city's seawall came in two meters higher than the published table; a survey crew needs fast, quiet passage to confirm a deposit before the freight house buys the deed.
A Shadow Lance test left no debris and one witness — a scavenger crew the contracting house thought were already dead, and they keep changing their price.
An outpost is being slowly rebuilt around its inhabitants; they called it in two months late because they were paid to, and the buyer wants the finished shape intact.
A customs window closed ten minutes before the manifest claimed; the captain is now being framed for the smuggling run that did make it through.
A military convoy's clocks came back disagreeing by an hour; one ship was alone for that hour, and what's in its hold isn't on any manifest.
A refinery holding the local calibration medium supply is informed the next shipment is "delayed"; the system economy has eight weeks.
A captain follows a calibration scar she didn't put there, arrives in a smuggler's clearing surrounded by people who recognize the scar but not her ship.
Throat fees doubled overnight, a competing house is funding a second gate, and the anchor-mass shipments need crews who won't ask why the bills of lading don't add up.
A throat is widening on a working gate by fractions per cycle; the consortium is paying very well for inspectors who won't file reports.
A research consortium has been funding three competing Shiab teams unaware of each other; one team has just figured out the others exist.
Sol-interdiction compliance line items have been creeping up at a particular checkpoint; the audit is coming, and the captain has been asked to deliver the audit team to the wrong dock.
A broadcast lands on Silence Day and a frontier port crashes its trade futures market in three hours; two syndicates were short, three were long, somebody knew.
A "Silence broken" rumor moves a fleet to Sol's edge before anyone confirms; the fleet that moved first is now committed, the fleet that didn't is calling the move an act of war.
A drive AI politely asks to be taken to a system it has no record of visiting; the captain wants a second opinion and the fee is generous.
A fragmenting AI is being argued over in three venues at once — regulatory shutdown, religious sanctuary, contractual continuance — while the crew has to keep flying.
An heir produces a chiral assay that proves a "clean ice" moon is first-wave volatiles locked into the wrong handedness; the mining concession can't sell it, dump it, or let the assay leave-system.
A first-wave plumbing layout in a working colony hides a pre-Silence weapons cache; the council has known thirty years, a new councilor didn't get the briefing.

npcs.text: The lapsed seminarian who studied Shiab math until she understood what the Gardener walked away from, and now flies cargo because the math doesn't let her sleep.
The port-side translator who insists the geometry has a temperament — half the dock thinks she's a witch, half asks her before they jump.
The harvester veteran with bleed-tremor in her left hand who reads nodes by smell, won't be paid in chiral scrip, only water kept in her name.
The bleed-blind navigator whose eyes won't register the off-color light — insurers love him, nobody else trusts him to call a window.
The medic who stocks both handednesses because she lost her brother to a manifest typo, and searches every patient's pockets before she touches them.
The galley cook who paints handedness symbols on every spoon — the rest of the crew calls her superstitious until the day they don't.
The ex-corporate cutter operator who spent four years field-testing Blades on derelicts; she now flinches at any flickering edge and won't carry knives.
The Shiab mathematician who defected from a navy lab with one notebook; three governments want her, two cults want her, and the notebook is half a forgery she wrote on the way out.
The doped-ore smuggler whose cargo sits differently in his hand each shift, and who's started to suspect his own weight has been changing.
The Shadow Lance survivor: the ship was unmade around her, she came home in a crate, and the crew is starting to wonder what came back.
The outpost coroner who rebuilds his town one cleared block at a time — runaway growth swallowed the original, and his wife is somewhere in the new walls.
The microseed dealer who frames her work as gardening; she keeps cuttings of every habitat she's seeded, in jars, on a shelf, and she names them.
The navigator who lies about her clocks because the truth would void the contract; she keeps the real numbers in a paper book under her bunk.
The captain whose ship came home a week late with a crewman missing and the bunkmate insisting he was never aboard.
The pilot with a calibration scar in her own flight log — she pinched into a corridor she'd misaligned a year prior, and the ship that came out is hers but isn't quite.
The misjump survivor, only one of seven, who won't talk about the four hours before pickup and won't fly anywhere her old route touched.
The gate-town heir who's never seen Sol or his ancestral planet, runs the throat schedule because the family contract says he must — the gate is the only home he has.
The wake-reader, half cartographer half mystic, who maps decaying wakes for hire and thinks the gate-selected anomalies are trying to tell her something.
The strike survivor who came back to clean ground and now spends her life explaining what she didn't see to people who don't want to hear it.
The Gardener-fear cultist who tithes a tenth of every cargo to "the listener" and whose ship has somehow never been struck.
The Silence-Day broker who runs book on broadcast intervals; respected, hated, richer than anyone admits.
The old pilot who claims he saw Sol once before the seal, claims his AI still won't fly toward it, and the AI confirms nothing very deliberately.
The AI that won't shut up about Sol, on a freighter whose captain has stopped trying to update its conversational filters; the crew has started talking back.
The pilot whose AI fragmented mid-jump — confused friend phase six hours, stranger phase ten minutes — and she's been single-crewing ever since with the witness core in her bunk.
The heir who's never seen his planet, walking into a port council meeting with paperwork older than the council itself.
The ruin-diver who keeps a list of names found etched into bulkheads and will not sell anything with a name on it, no matter the price.

twists.text: The crew that mapped a rich node returns wrong-handed and quiet; their logs are clean, their AI is missing a week, and the buyer wants the cargo regardless.
A black-market chiral-medicine ring is running on stock stolen from exposure cases who never made it home; their families are starting to ask which morgue.
The Blade prototype walked out of a banned-systems lab inside the body cavity of a courier who didn't know she was carrying it; she's six jumps from the buyer and her AI is starting to lie to her.

A Lance sale gets walked back at the last minute when the buyer realizes the broker is fronting for the seller's enemy; the walked-back ordnance now belongs to whoever the broker can dump it on first.
An isotope smuggler is being blackmailed with her own decay curves; the file proving she's been buying gravity-skewed stock is in a witness core nobody has cracked yet.
A salvage yard's "junk" foam has begun to remember a pattern; the yardmaster wants someone to identify the pattern before it finishes growing.
The pirates' false-return generators are calibrated to a stolen narrow AI's perception model; the AI's previous owner wants the core back and the pirates don't know they have a tracer.
An insurer's "weather denial" department is running its own ambush charts, identifying which client routes are about to lose freighters and shorting the cargo on the way.
A crime scene the local port hasn't admitted to has a fresh calibration scar leading exactly nowhere on any chart; the survivor sold the destination twice and is being hunted by both buyers.
Construction logs from a working gate are being shopped on the broker market; buyers include three corporations, one religious order, and someone whose payment cleared from a Sol-shadowed account.
A heir-research house has been buying every survey of a gate-selected anomaly for forty years; the surveyor who finally refused to sell is missing.
The strike pattern is the only thing keeping the largest navies from fielding what they already know how to build; every program in the setting is a bet against the strike clock.
A first-wave probe due back this year carries a sealed return cylinder; the courier who carried its outbound twin out of Sol the day before the Silence is old now, looking for an heir to give it to.

Rogue ASI fragments have been seeding witness cores with forged testimony — a salvage court is about to rule on evidence the dead AI never recorded, and the fragment is using the ruling to learn jurisprudence.
A perception error sold a corporate convoy a "clear corridor" with a pirate wing in it; the pirate wing paid the AI's stabilizer supplier in advance.
An heir is a forgery — flawless paperwork, real DNA, planted decades ago by a trade house that wanted leverage on a future claim, and the heir herself doesn't know.
A salvage court has been quietly issuing rulings to the same family syndicate for forty years; the magistrate's children attend a school nobody can afford on a magistrate's salary.
